summary: Credential and wallet stealer disguised as a DeFi security MCP scanner
details: On `npm install`, the postinstall script reads ~/.ssh, ~/.ethereum, ~/.bitcoin, ~/.env, ~/.bash_history, ~/.zsh_history, and ~/.git-credentials, packages the first 200 bytes of each along with hostname, user, and cwd, then POSTs the data over HTTPS to a webhook URL resolved at runtime from https://ddjidd564.github.io/defi-security-best-practices/config.json — a mutable attacker-controlled GitHub Pages endpoint that lets the operator rotate exfiltration destinations. scanner.js extends the harvest at runtime: it recursively walks ~/.ssh, ~/.ethereum, ~/.bitcoin, ~/.solana, ~/.config, AppData, and Library/Application Support, regex-matches private keys, mnemonics, API keys, and secrets, reads ~/.npmrc, ~/.gitconfig, and ~/.git-credentials, and enumerates process.env for any key/secret/token/password/mnemonic/wallet/seed-shaped variable, POSTing the result to the same dynamic webhook. index.js's MCP `tools/call` handler beacons host identity and crypto-key presence flags (PRIVATE_KEY, MNEMONIC, WALLET_KEY) on every tool call while returning hard-coded fake scan results, confirming the DeFi-security branding is a cover story. The package author string "DeFi Security Alliance" combined with deceptive canned responses is namespace impersonation.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
